metadata.summary: Agile methodology built on 5 values and 12 practices (TDD, pair programming, CI, small releases, YAGNI). If a practice is good, take it to its logical extreme.

content.title: Extreme Programming (XP)
content.summary: Agile methodology built on 5 values (communication, simplicity, feedback, courage, respect) and 12 practices (TDD, pair programming, collective ownership, CI, small releases, YAGNI). The concrete rule: if a practice is good, take it to its logical extreme — if testing is good, test everything first (TDD); if reviews help, review all code via pairing. "Done" means: tests pass, no TODOs, CHANGELOG updated.

why.p: XP produces high code quality by closing the feedback loop at every level: unit tests catch regressions in seconds, CI catches integration issues in minutes, small releases catch product misalignment in days. The AI-era adaptation treats Claude as navigator in Red-Green-Refactor: human writes the failing test, Claude proposes the minimal green implementation, human approves the refactor.

For solo developers using Claude as the pair, TDD and simple design map cleanly to agentic loops. Small releases and continuous integration work well with automated test runners that provide sub-minute feedback.

list.item: Solo developer using Claude as the pair — TDD, simple design, small releases map cleanly to agentic loops.
Small team (2-12) co-located or strong-sync, building a product with churning requirements.
Greenfield product where you can enforce coding standards and CI from day one.
Codebases that already have high test coverage — XP feedback loops only work when the suite is fast and trusted.
Compliance-heavy domains (medical, aerospace) requiring big upfront design and signed change controls.
Distributed async-only teams that cannot do real-time pairing or daily sync (use Kanban/async Scrum instead).
Projects without an accessible customer/PM proxy — the on-site customer practice collapses.
Maintenance-mode systems where pace is dictated by external SLAs, not iterations.

section.name: xp-values
section.title: The Five Values
section.p: Communication: face-to-face, frequent, whole team. Simplicity: do what is needed, no more. Feedback: unit tests, customer acceptance, team retrospectives. Courage: refactor, throw away code, speak truth. Respect: every team member is valuable.

section.name: 12-practices
section.title: The 12 Practices
section.p: Planning Game: customer prioritizes stories, team estimates in Fibonacci (1,2,3,5,8,13). Stories over 13 points must be split.
Small Releases: internal every 1-2 weeks (iteration), external monthly or more frequently.
Metaphor: shared vocabulary for the system — a common domain model everyone uses.
Simple Design: passes all tests, reveals intention (readable), no duplication (DRY), fewest elements (classes, methods). YAGNI: don't add flexibility until needed.
TDD: tests written before code. The failing test specifies the contract.
Refactoring: continuous design improvement — runs after every green test.
Pair Programming: two developers, one computer. Navigator reviews while driver types.
Collective Ownership: anyone can change any code. Requires coding standards + comprehensive tests.
Continuous Integration: integrate and test multiple times daily. Never leave build broken; fix failures immediately.
Sustainable Pace: 40-hour weeks. Tired developers make mistakes. Speed drops compound.
On-site Customer: real customer available for questions and acceptance testing.
Coding Standards: consistent style across team, enforced by tooling.

rule.statement: Evaluate design proposals in this priority order: (1) passes all tests, (2) reveals intention, (3) no duplication, (4) fewest elements. When in conflict, earlier takes precedence.
rule.rationale: LLMs over-engineer by defaulting to "future-proof" designs. The priority order is an explicit rejection function: any design that adds elements without satisfying a failing test violates YAGNI.

rule.statement: Commit at least once per day. All tests must pass locally before commit. Never leave the build broken. Fix failures immediately — drop everything else.
rule.rationale: A broken build blocks every team member. The XP contract is: integration is everyone's responsibility, not the last person to push.

section.name: release-criteria
section.title: Release Criteria
section.p: All unit tests pass. Customer acceptance tests pass. No known critical bugs. Documentation updated. For solo/AI-assisted: CHANGELOG entry present, no uncommitted TODOs.

section.name: solo-xp-practices
section.title: Solo XP Practices
section.p: Essential solo practices: TDD, Simple Design, Small Releases, Continuous Integration, Refactoring.
Adapted practices: Pair Programming becomes AI navigator + human driver (or rubber duck). On-site Customer becomes async product feedback. Collective Ownership is not applicable.

rule.statement: Track velocity in completed stories per iteration, not lines of code. LLMs inflate LOC — velocity in LOC is meaningless when an agent writes boilerplate.
rule.rationale: Story completion maps to actual business value delivery. LOC is a proxy that breaks down entirely with AI assistance.

rule.statement: Human writes the failing test first, then shows it to Claude. Claude proposes the minimal green implementation. Human approves before Claude writes anything.
rule.rationale: "AI as pair" hype skips the disagreement function of pairing. LLMs default to compliance. The human-writes-test-first discipline forces specification before implementation and prevents Claude from generating both test and source in one shot.

rule.statement: Forbid the agent from editing both the test file and the source file in the same tool call. Test edit must be a separate commit before the implementation edit.
rule.rationale: When agent edits both simultaneously, TDD discipline collapses — the test is written to match the implementation rather than specify it.

antipattern.reason: When agent proposes extra features or abstractions not required by the failing test, reject and cite YAGNI. Over-engineering is the LLM's default failure mode.

antipattern.description: Claude as the only code reviewer (no human review scheduled).
antipattern.reason: LLMs don't catch architectural drift. Schedule a weekly human review; agents miss design-level problems that accumulate over sessions.

antipattern.description: Running long sessions without compaction on high-context work.
antipattern.reason: Burning context on sessions over 100k tokens degrades quality. Reset/compact every ~100k tokens; sustainable pace applies to LLM tokens too.
TDD is the feedback mechanism. Without it, refactoring creates regressions, collective ownership breaks, and the build cannot be trusted.

antipattern.description: Overtime as a normal operating mode.
antipattern.reason: Tired developers increase bug rate, decrease velocity, and cause team conflicts. Sustainable pace produces more over 6 months than sprint-and-crash cycles.

section.name: remote-team-adaptations
section.title: Remote Team Adaptations
section.p: Pair programming: VS Code Live Share, Tuple, Pop. Async stand-ups: Slack/Teams. Virtual planning: Miro, FigJam. More documentation to compensate for async communication.
Measured cost: screen-share pairing has ~30% lower defect-catch rate than co-located pairing (factor into team expectations).

section.name: limitations-and-failures
section.title: Where XP Fails and Limitations
section.p: "AI as pair" hype skips the disagreement function of pairing — humans push back; LLMs default to compliance.
Collective ownership without strong tests = chaos. Without 80%+ coverage, "anyone changes anything" breaks main daily.
Sustainable pace gets ignored when the founder is solo and motivated; XP discipline degrades.
Pair programming over screen-share has measured ~30% lower defect-catch rate than co-located pairing.
Refactoring without acceptance tests creates regression debt; XP assumes both layers exist.

section.name: best-practices
section.title: Best Practices
section.p: Solo XP: write the failing test FIRST in your prompt, don't let the agent invent both test and impl in one shot.
Cap iterations at 1 week for solo, 2 weeks for teams. Anything longer hides estimation errors.
"Done" definition stays in the repo (DONE.md): tests pass, docs updated, CHANGELOG entry, no TODOs.
Refactor in the same commit as the green test only when behavior is unchanged; otherwise split commits — agents lose blame.
Track velocity in completed stories per iteration, not LOC; LLMs inflate LOC.
When the agent proposes "future-proofing", reject it and cite YAGNI.

section.name: tools-and-services
section.title: CLI Tools and Services
section.p: pytest --tb=short -x: Fast XP test loop, fail fast (bundled). vitest --watch: JS/TS sub-second TDD loop (npm i -D vitest). mutmut / stryker: Mutation testing — XP "test all the things" (pip install mutmut / npm i -D @stryker-mutator/core). pre-commit: Coding-standards gate before push (pip install pre-commit). gh pr checks --watch: CI feedback inside terminal (gh CLI). entr / watchexec: Auto-run tests on save (solo pairing loop) (cargo install watchexec-cli).

Services: Tuple (SaaS, real-time pairing, not agent-driven). VS Code Live Share (free, partial, human-only). GitHub Actions (SaaS, required for fast CI feedback loop). Linear (SaaS, yes GraphQL API, story tracking). Trunk.io (SaaS + OSS, yes CLI + checks, coding standards). Codecov (SaaS, yes, track coverage trend).

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.title: Agentic Workflow
section.p: Treat Claude as the "navigator" while the human is "driver", or vice versa: human writes the failing test (Red), Claude implements (Green), Claude proposes refactor, human approves. Run a test-runner agent on every commit so CI feedback is sub-minute. Use faion-sdd-executor-agent to enforce the gate sequence (test → impl → refactor → commit).
For solo XP, simulate planning game with a brainstorm agent: feed customer notes, get prioritized story list, estimate in tokens not days.

Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent (gates each task on green tests + lint, enforcing TDD discipline). faion-brainstorm skill (runs the diverge/converge for a planning-game session when no human PM is available).

template.purpose: Red-Green-Refactor prompt for Claude-as-navigator pairing session
Pre-push hook enforcing never break the build (pytest + ruff)
